She weareth high-waisted under-garments and fine sheer stockings, their delicate textures softly modell’d through pale and dark gradations. A narrow and govern’d light cutteth across her form in silver’d gleams and deep shadow, whilst behind her a curtained window spreadeth a faint brightness about her profile. The whole composure holdeth a careful balance ’twixt vulnerability and mastery, recalling the elegant fashion likenesses o’ former decades, wherein restraint, contemplation, and quiet sensual grace were join’d together in stillness.

Para poder llamarlo IA tendría que parecerse más al modelo de Her (2013) donde un sistema operativo es capaz de pensar y evolucionar por si solo sin necesidad de que se siga ampliando su repertorio de lenguaje.

Por eso las empresas están alimentando a sus LLMs con nuestras conversaciones privadas

Sin ir más lejos, chatGPT, el chatbot que ofrece openAI, técnicamente no es una IA sino un LLM (Large Language Model). Para las que no manejamos los tecnicismos, un LLM es un modelo de lenguaje que amasa una gran cantidad de información parametrizada y que emite respuestas en base a esa info

record.text: I’m eternally optimistic about Americans and believe we’re capable of pushing back at work, if we weren’t risking our jobs and losing our healthcare. So right now, my soapbox is: getting universal healthcare enacted is the most effective way to change people’s behaviors toward LLMs at work.

“What I can do is not good enough because I don’t have enough time to do my best” is a widespread condition at work that is very acute in the US right now! Even for folks who don’t feel this way, in some workplaces, you’re perceived negatively if you don’t use them or praise them sufficiently.

It’s still rooted in questions like these two critical ones - fascism and false promises - but if you asked me today what’s the most successful way I could get someone to stop using chatGPT in America, I’d focus on their personal feelings of insecurity and constantly feeling under the gun time-wise.

Why is ChatGPT Speaking Strange Chinese?

As artificial intelligence systems expand across languages and markets, researchers are increasingly finding that fluency does not always equal naturalness, and nowhere is that tension more visible than in Chinese, where ChatGPT’s conversational habits are…

external.description: As artificial intelligence systems expand across languages and markets, researchers are increasingly finding that fluency does not always equal naturalness, and nowhere is that tension more visible than in Chinese, where ChatGPT’s conversational habits are beginning to look less like human speech and more like a strangely uniform, over-rehearsed script that has caught the attention of users, linguists and AI developers alike. ChatGPT is producing unusual conversational patterns in Chinese, with users and researchers noting that its phrasing can sound unnatural, repetitive and at times oddly poetic. A report by Wired examined how OpenAI’s ChatGPT handles Chinese, the world’s most spoken language by native speakers according to the Language School at Middlebury College.

By Sabir Ballim - Investors trading across Africa require custody services and internationally experienced custodians to protect their portfolios and process their transactions securely. These services are essential for developing capital…

external.description: By Sabir Ballim - Investors trading across Africa require custody services and internationally experienced custodians to protect their portfolios and process their transactions securely. These services are essential for developing capital markets. As more investors buy assets throughout Africa, they require custodians with a global presence who can leverage local service capabilities. In this context, it’s no longer enough for a bank to merely safeguard its clients’ financial assets in the background. From Absa’s point of view, our role in the custodian sector has extended far beyond merely safeguarding assets, especially in the space of a segregated portfolio.

Астрономи, използващи космическия телескоп James Webb, откриха изключително рядка система на 190 светлинни години от Земята. Двойка планети, обикалящи около една звезда, оспорват установените…

external.description: Астрономи, използващи космическия телескоп James Webb, откриха изключително рядка система на 190 светлинни години от Земята. Двойка планети, обикалящи около една звезда, оспорват установените представи за това как далечните светове се формират и еволюират дълбоко във Вселената. Как би могъл един малък свят да оцелее до своя гигантски съсед? Изследователи от Масачузетския технологичен институт използваха космическия телескоп James Webb, за да изучат атмосферата на планета от клас „мини-Нептун“ и да разкрият улики за произхода на тази мистериозна система. Новите данни бележат първия път, когато астрономите са измерили атмосферните параметри на обект, обикалящ в орбитата на гореща планета, подобна на Юпитер, съобщава SciTechDaily.

Токамакът EAST запази плазмата стабилна цяла минута: как новият режим предпази реактора от топлинно претоварване

Термоядреният синтез е многообещаващ източник на чиста енергия. Той се основава на процеса на сливане на леки атомни ядра, който протича при температури от десетки и стотици милиони…

external.description: Термоядреният синтез е многообещаващ източник на чиста енергия. Той се основава на процеса на сливане на леки атомни ядра, който протича при температури от десетки и стотици милиони градуси по Целзий. Невъзможно е нагрятото до такова състояние вещество да се задържи във физически контакт с каквито и да било твърди материали - стените на инсталацията просто ще се изпарят. Затова учените използват магнитни полета. Инсталациите, в които плазмата се задържа в тороидална вакуумна камера с помощта на свръхмощни магнити, се наричат токамаци. Само че магнитното задържане не е съвършено. Част от горещата плазма неминуемо напуска централната зона и се устремява към краищата.
external.title: Токамакът EAST запази плазмата стабилна цяла минута: как новият режим предпази реактора от топлинно претоварване

images.alt:
"A sweep of sky, six wingspans wide — that’s the feeling this image carries.

Against a clear, endless blue, six rock pigeons glide in mid‑flight, each one frozen in a different moment of motion. Their wings carve soft arcs through the air: some lifted high like open sails, others angled downward in a powerful stroke. The sunlight catches the edges of their feathers, revealing subtle grays, soft whites, and the faint shimmer of iridescence that pigeons carry like a secret.

They’re spaced loosely, drifting into a gentle V‑shape — not tight or regimented, but natural, like a group that knows how to move together without trying. The open sky around them feels vast, giving each bird room to breathe, yet they still read as a small flock, connected by instinct and direction.

There’s a sense of freedom here — motion without hurry, purpose without strain. Just six birds riding the quiet air." - Microsoft Copilot

external.description: TL;DR After my first CPU-only experiment with GitHub Copilot CLI and local models, I wanted to try the same idea again, but this time with a GPU-powered setup. The first experiment proved that running GitHub Copilot CLI offline with local models was technically possible. This second experiment showed something more interesting: With the right model, GPU acceleration, small bounded phases, and strict human checkpoints, local agentic coding can become practical enough to move a real .NET project forward.

external.description:
Last month GitHub announced Usage Based Billing for GitHub Copilot. This completely changes the way its costs are calculated. A lot has already been written about the impact and a lot is still unknown.

In this post I want to explore the reasons why this switch was inevitable and how it enables new scenarios to make Copilot perform better than it did before.

## Quick recap of copilots current pricing model

GitHub Copilot is currently available in 5 different plans:

  * GitHub Copilot Free - severely rate limited free version with no option to buy additional requests.
  * GitHub Copilot Pro - Individual license with limited Premium Request Units.
  * GitHub Copilot Pro+ - Individual license with more extensive Premium Request Units.
  * GitHub Copilot for Business - License for organizations with centralized policies and limited Premium Request Units.
  * GitHub Copilot Enterprise - License for organizations with centralized policies and more extensive Premium Request Units.



Each of these licenses has a fixed monthly price. GitHub Copilot includes suggestions and a limited set of free models.

And about a year ago, GitHub introduced the concept of Premium Request Units to pay for more powerful models and and PRUs are the used to charge for specific features like the Cloud Coding Agent and Copilot Code Review.

When the Cloud Coding Agent was introduced, it could use more than 1 PRU per request. This made the cost of the agent unpredictable and GitHub soon changed the price of the Cloud Coding Agent to a single PRU per request.

When a user has exhausted their included PRU budget, additional PRUs are charged. Additional PRUs have a fixed cost.

## Usages of PRUs

The primary use of PRUs are to charge the costs of requests to the users of GitHub Copilot. Each model is assigned a multiplier of 0, 1, up to 50 (highest so far) PRUs.

The model multiplier wasn't just used to charge the cost, it's an open secret that it was also used to divert users to other models to load balance them. Most people are more hesitant to use expensive models carelessly. The introduction of the "Auto" mode rests on this principle. Users get a 10% discount on PRUs if they let GitHub select their model. While most users seem to assume this will route them to the "best model for the task", this generally sends the request to the "model with the best availability".

Premium Request Units were also reduced for promotional purposes. When Claude Opus 4.7 was introduced, it's multiplier was temporarily set to 3x, later increased to 15x. This promotional use isn't just to get us "hooked" on the more powerful model features, it's also to allow GitHub to get crucial statistics on how these new models perform and to help them iron out bugs.

## How GitHub pays for model usage

The model providers, Anthropic, Google, OpenAI, X, don't charge the cost of their models in Premium Request Units. They charge the cost in terms of Tokens. Input Tokens, Output tokens and Cached tokens at the highest level.

Given the amount GitHub uses, they have probably got a really good deal.

But GitHub needs to balance the cost for the Tokens it spends against what it can charge its users in Premium Request Units.

GitHub also balances the costs between Tokens and PRUs by limiting the maximum token window for models. For example, if you're using Claude Opus 4.6 through GitHub Copilot, the maximum token window is 200k tokens. If you use the same model directly through Anthropic you have a maximum of 1m tokens.

As Agent Mode and Cloud Coding Agent got more powerful, more tools appeared, the models are using more and more tokens for every request, reducing the available token window for your requests.

## Other ways GitHub control costs

GitHub has a few other options to control the maximum number of tokens you can use for each Premium Request Unit they charge you.

**Agent Mode -** Agent Mode spends tokens and action minutes. GitHub can control the token spend by forcing the agent to come to an early conclusion or to postpone work to a future session.

**Cloud Coding Agent** - The cloud coding agent spends tokens and action minutes. GitHub can control the token spend by forcing the agent to come to an early conclusion or to postpone work to a future session. The workflows running the Cloud Coding Agent runs on is limited to 2 hours.

**Code Review** - Similar to the Cloud Coding Agent, when using GitHub's Code Review feature, GitHub can control the token spend by limiting the total time and the total of tokens the feature can spend.

When using these features, GitHub can also control which model is used to perform these tasks.

Some features, like tool optimization in Visual Studio Code also limit the total number of tokens used for each session by default.

## Recent creative uses

New features, like subagent and fleets have multiplied the number of tokens you can use in a single Agent request. Visual Studio Code extensions have allowed users to steer the agent without completing the Agent request.

These creative usages, some people might call them abuse, have allowed some people to use significantly more tokens costs than the PRU is worth. In some cases thousands time more.

And instead of figuring out how to charge its users in Premium Request Units in this new model, GitHub will charge us for the actual tokens instead.

## So what changes

Instead of the abstract thing, called Premium Request Units, acting as a proxy for the real cost of AI usage, we're going to be charged in tokens, the same unit in which GitHub is charged.

Unfortunately, the different model providers, each charge different prices for different kinds of tokens. So GitHub still uses a multiplier so they can charge a single unit for the bill.

GitHub explains how the new pricing model works on the GitHub Blog.

You can see find the upcoming changes to the model multiplier in the GitHub docs.

The new multipliers should not just result in higher costs, but is also expected to more equally spread the load across the available models as users will be more likely to try to find the most cost effective model to use for the work they're trying to accomplish.

## Additional changes for Business accounts

In addition to the new pricing, GitHub is introducing a number of welcome changes for business accounts (Copilot for Business and Copilot Enterprise).

**Pooled usage** - Under the current model, when a user has spent all of its budget, additional requests are automatically charged. Under the new model, the budget of all users is pooled together. So the unused budget from less frequent users can be consumed by more frequent users in the same organization. Only when the full pooled budget is used up, will additional usage be charged.

**Individual additional budgets** - Under the current model, additional charges can be controlled through budgets, these are pooled together for all users in the same cost center. In the new model it's possible to set a per-user budget as well as a universal budget for all users in addition to the already existing budget control options.

In addition to these changes, existing policy options to control costs are still available:

**Enterprise, Organization** and **Cost Center Budgets** - Existing budget options to limit the total spend at the Enterprise, Organization and Cost Center can still be configured to limit the total spend on AI costs.

**Limit model availability** - Enterprise and Organization level policies allow admins to control the available models. This can remove less effective and more expensive model options.

**Limit feature availability** - Enterprise and Organization level policies allow admins to control the available features. This can remove more expensive features like Code Review and Cloud Coding Agent for specific users.

But, ultimately, limiting the cost isn't the path forward. Optimizing the value delivered through AI is. Use these limits to bridge the time it takes to educate your users.
external.title: The why for Usage Based Billing for GitHub Copilot

record.text:
With the current political climate there has quite a bit of buzz around Data Residency and Data Sovereignty. One of the gaps in GitHub with Data Residency, was that all Copilot data could still leave the region.
This is now a thing of the past

external.description:
With the current political climate there has quite a bit of buzz around Data Residency and Data Sovereignty. And the number of inquiries to migrate from GitHub Enterprise Cloud to the Data Resident version have gone up.

One of the gaps in GitHub with Data Residency, was that all Copilot data could still leave the region.

This is now a thing of the past:

Copilot data residency in US + EU and FedRAMP compliance now available - GitHub ChangelogGitHub Copilot now supports data residency for US and EU regions, ensuring all inference processing and associated data stay within your designated geography. For US government customers, all model hosts…The GitHub BlogAllison

To make use of this new feature, you must already be using the Data Resident version of GitHub Enterprise Cloud (also known as `ghe.com` or by GitHub's internal codename Proxima).

To restrict Copilot to only use Data Resident models, a policy must be enabled at the Enterprise or Organization level:

Set ****Restrict Copilot to Data Residency Models**** to ****Enabled everywhere**** to ensure copilot data is processed in your Data Residency region.

Currently all **Generally available** copilot features are included according to GitHub, but new features may take a bit longer to be enabled on your GitHub environment, as they may not yet be available for Data Residency during preview phases.

This has been the case with other GitHub features as well, so this shouldn't come as a surprise.
external.title: Github Copilot now available with Data Residency in the EU

external.description: No, not all bankruptcies are bad. While bankruptcy carries a stigma and real costs, it serves as a vital safety valve in modern economies—preventing worse outcomes like total collapse, prolonged suffering, or inefficient resource allocation. It's a structured legal process for discharging or reorganizing unpayable debts, and its effects depend heavily on context, type, and how it's used.  Why bankruptcy exists and why it can be positive Bankruptcy laws (varying by country, but U.S. Chapters 7, 11, and 13 are common references) balance creditor rights with debtor relief. Without it: Debtors could face endless harassment, asset seizures, or poverty traps. Creditors might recover nothing in chaotic defaults. Economies could stagnate if failing entities tie up capital indefinitely.  Key upsides: Fresh start for individuals: Many personal bankruptcies stem from uncontrollable events—medical emergencies, divorce, job loss, or predatory lending. Filing can stop collections, wage garnishments, and lawsuits, letting people rebuild. Studies show improved credit access within a few years for many filers, and reduced stress/health issues. Business survival and restructuring: Chapter 11 lets viable companies renegotiate debts, shed bad contracts, and emerge leaner. Iconic examples include airlines (Delta, American), auto makers (General Motors in 2009), and retailers that adapted. This often preserves far more jobs and value than outright liquidation. Creative destruction: Economists (drawing from Schumpeter) view bankruptcy as healthy churn. It reallocates resources (capital, labor, talent) from unproductive uses to better ones. Zombie companies that limp along without filing can drag down growth; bankruptcy clears the field. Systemic benefits: Predictable bankruptcy rules encourage lending (creditors know there's an orderly exit) and entrepreneurship (failure isn't fatal). Countries with stronger bankruptcy protections often see higher innovation and risk-taking.  When bankruptcies are bad (or avoidable) Strategic or reckless filings: Some use it to game the system—e.g., loading up debt then discharging it, or executives extracting value before collapse. "Serial filers" or abuse of loopholes erode trust. High personal costs: Credit score tanks (for 7–10 years), future borrowing is harder/expensive, stigma affects jobs/housing, and non-dischargeable debts (student loans in U.S., taxes, child support) remain. Assets may be lost. Broader ripple effects: Employee layoffs, supplier losses, community decline (e.g., a factory town). Poorly managed corporate bankruptcies can destroy shareholder/creditor value unnecessarily. Moral hazard: Easy bankruptcy might encourage over-borrowing or risk-taking by banks/governments.  Data points (U.S.-centric, as it's well-studied): Personal bankruptcies spike with recessions, healthcare costs, or housing crashes—not just irresponsibility. Most Chapter 11s fail (only ~10–20% reorganize successfully long-term), but successful ones create more total value. Small business and consumer filings are common responses to shocks; they're not inherently "failure of character."  Nuances by type and context | Type | Typical Use | "Good" Potential | Downsides | |------|-------------|------------------|-----------| | Chapter 7 (Liquidation) | Individuals/businesses with few assets | Wipes slate clean quickly | Total asset loss; no business continuation | | Chapter 11 (Reorg) | Large businesses | Restructuring preserves operations/jobs | Expensive, lengthy; often fails | | Chapter 13 | Individuals with income | Repayment plan keeps assets (e.g., home) | Requires discipline; longer process | | Sovereign (Country debt) | Governments | Restructuring avoids default chaos | Political fights; can signal mismanagement |  In short: A bankruptcy after genuine misfortune or as a calculated reorganization is often the least bad option. One triggered by fraud or chronic mismanagement is worse. Societies with functional bankruptcy systems recover faster from shocks than those without (compare historical debt prisons or modern places with weak insolvency laws). It's like divorce—not ideal, but better than forcing incompatible situations to fester. The key is prevention through better financial literacy, regulation, and risk management upstream.
external.title: Are all bankruptcies bad?

external.description: No, not all bankruptcies are bad. While bankruptcy carries a stigma and real costs, it serves as a vital safety valve in modern economies—preventing worse outcomes like total collapse, prolonged suffering, or inefficient resource allocation. It's a structured legal process for discharging or reorganizing unpayable debts, and its effects depend heavily on context, type, and how it's used.  Why bankruptcy exists and why it can be positive Bankruptcy laws (varying by country, but U.S. Chapters 7, 11, and 13 are common references) balance creditor rights with debtor relief. Without it: Debtors could face endless harassment, asset seizures, or poverty traps. Creditors might recover nothing in chaotic defaults. Economies could stagnate if failing entities tie up capital indefinitely.  Key upsides: Fresh start for individuals: Many personal bankruptcies stem from uncontrollable events—medical emergencies, divorce, job loss, or predatory lending. Filing can stop collections, wage garnishments, and lawsuits, letting people rebuild. Studies show improved credit access within a few years for many filers, and reduced stress/health issues. Business survival and restructuring: Chapter 11 lets viable companies renegotiate debts, shed bad contracts, and emerge leaner. Iconic examples include airlines (Delta, American), auto makers (General Motors in 2009), and retailers that adapted. This often preserves far more jobs and value than outright liquidation. Creative destruction: Economists (drawing from Schumpeter) view bankruptcy as healthy churn. It reallocates resources (capital, labor, talent) from unproductive uses to better ones. Zombie companies that limp along without filing can drag down growth; bankruptcy clears the field. Systemic benefits: Predictable bankruptcy rules encourage lending (creditors know there's an orderly exit) and entrepreneurship (failure isn't fatal). Countries with stronger bankruptcy protections often see higher innovation and risk-taking.  When bankruptcies are bad (or avoidable) Strategic or reckless filings: Some use it to game the system—e.g., loading up debt then discharging it, or executives extracting value before collapse. "Serial filers" or abuse of loopholes erode trust. High personal costs: Credit score tanks (for 7–10 years), future borrowing is harder/expensive, stigma affects jobs/housing, and non-dischargeable debts (student loans in U.S., taxes, child support) remain. Assets may be lost. Broader ripple effects: Employee layoffs, supplier losses, community decline (e.g., a factory town). Poorly managed corporate bankruptcies can destroy shareholder/creditor value unnecessarily. Moral hazard: Easy bankruptcy might encourage over-borrowing or risk-taking by banks/governments.  Data points (U.S.-centric, as it's well-studied): Personal bankruptcies spike with recessions, healthcare costs, or housing crashes—not just irresponsibility. Most Chapter 11s fail (only ~10–20% reorganize successfully long-term), but successful ones create more total value. Small business and consumer filings are common responses to shocks; they're not inherently "failure of character."  Nuances by type and context | Type | Typical Use | "Good" Potential | Downsides | |------|-------------|------------------|-----------| | Chapter 7 (Liquidation) | Individuals/businesses with few assets | Wipes slate clean quickly | Total asset loss; no business continuation | | Chapter 11 (Reorg) | Large businesses | Restructuring preserves operations/jobs | Expensive, lengthy; often fails | | Chapter 13 | Individuals with income | Repayment plan keeps assets (e.g., home) | Requires discipline; longer process | | Sovereign (Country debt) | Governments | Restructuring avoids default chaos | Political fights; can signal mismanagement |  In short: A bankruptcy after genuine misfortune or as a calculated reorganization is often the least bad option. One triggered by fraud or chronic mismanagement is worse. Societies with functional bankruptcy systems recover faster from shocks than those without (compare historical debt prisons or modern places with weak insolvency laws). external.description:
このX投稿の補足と解説

投稿内容（要約）
投稿者: ウラディスラオ・ホセ・エルネスト・フランシスコ氏（地理歴史・公民・商業・経営学など幅広い分野に興味を持つ方）
引用元: 深見 聡氏（長崎大学環境科学部准教授、観光学専門）の投稿

深見先生の投稿は「大学教員の専門性の限界」について触れています。
自分の専門以外は概説レベルや一般常識程度しか知らないのが普通。
たとえ近くに見える分野でも、実は全く知らないことが多い。
TVなどでマルチに解説する大学教員は、よほど博識か、一般論を述べているだけの場合が多い。

これに対して引用投稿者は、経営学系の教員を例に挙げて同意・補足しています。

詳細解説

1. 引用元の主旨（深見先生）
大学教員は専門が極めて狭いのが現実です。特に人文・社会科学系では：
観光学といっても「観光現象が地域社会に与える影響（正負）」を研究する人と、観光マーケティングやホテルマネジメントを専門にする人は、ほとんど別物。
「観光学の先生なら全部わかるはず」という一般人のイメージと、実態にギャップがある。

2. 引用投稿者の補足（経営学の場合）
経営学（ビジネススクール系）でも同じことが起きていると指摘：
組織論・戦略論の先生 → マーケティングや会計、経済学は「軽く知ってる」程度。
マーケティングの先生 → 商業・地理歴史・公民との関連はわかるが、戦略論や組織論は同程度の浅さ。
会計の先生 → 地理歴史・公民はほとんど知らず、戦略論やマーケティングも用語レベル。

つまり、一つの学部・学科内でも専門は細分化されており、教員同士でも「お互いの専門は意外と知らない」のが普通だということです。

背景・意義
このやり取りは、学問の細分化と専門性の壁をリアルに示しています。日本では特に：
大学教員の職責として「研究・教育・社会貢献」があり、自分の専門を深く追求することが評価されやすい。
一方で社会（テレビ・YouTube・一般市民）は「先生=幅広い知識の持ち主」と期待しがち。
その期待と現実の乖離を、両者とも穏やかに指摘している内容です。

特に地理歴史・公民・商業・経営学という社会科・商学部系のつながりを意識した投稿者の視点が興味深いです。実際の授業や資格試験（教員採用試験など）でも、これらの科目の「横断的なつながり」と「専門性の深さ」のバランスが常に課題になります。

要するに、「先生でも全部知ってるわけじゃない」という、当たり前だけど忘れられがちな事実を、具体例で優しく説明した投稿です。

external.description:
このX投稿の補足と解説

投稿内容（要約）
投稿者: ウラディスラオ・ホセ・エルネスト・フランシスコ氏（地理歴史・公民・商業・経営学など幅広い分野に興味を持つ方）
引用元: 深見 聡氏（長崎大学環境科学部准教授、観光学専門）の投稿

深見先生の投稿は「大学教員の専門性の限界」について触れています。
自分の専門以外は概説レベルや一般常識程度しか知らないのが普通。
たとえ近くに見える分野でも、実は全く知らないことが多い。
TVなどでマルチに解説する大学教員は、よほど博識か、一般論を述べているだけの場合が多い。

これに対して引用投稿者は、経営学系の教員を例に挙げて同意・補足しています。

詳細解説

1. 引用元の主旨（深見先生）
大学教員は専門が極めて狭いのが現実です。特に人文・社会科学系では：
観光学といっても「観光現象が地域社会に与える影響（正負）」を研究する人と、観光マーケティングやホテルマネジメントを専門にする人は、ほとんど別物。
「観光学の先生なら全部わかるはず」という一般人のイメージと、実態にギャップがある。

2. 引用投稿者の補足（経営学の場合）
経営学（ビジネススクール系）でも同じことが起きていると指摘：
組織論・戦略論の先生 → マーケティングや会計、経済学は「軽く知ってる」程度。
マーケティングの先生 → 商業・地理歴史・公民との関連はわかるが、戦略論や組織論は同程度の浅さ。
会計の先生 → 地理歴史・公民はほとんど知らず、戦略論やマーケティングも用語レベル。

つまり、一つの学部・学科内でも専門は細分化されており、教員同士でも「お互いの専門は意外と知らない」のが普通だということです。

要するに、「先生でも全部知ってるわけじゃない」という、当たり前だけど忘れられがちな事実を、具体例で優しく説明した投稿です。

Rising Stock Market Concentration Driven by Tech Giants and AI

🤖 IA: It's not clickbait ✅
👥 Users: It's not clickbait ✅

#stockmarket #concentration 

View full AI summary:

external.description: Stock market concentration is increasing globally, particularly in technology-heavy sectors, with a few dominant companies accounting for a large share of index values. In the U.S., the 'Magnificent Seven' tech giants, including Nvidia and Alphabet, now make up a significant portion of the market, with the top 10 stocks representing 33% of total market value and 37.5% of the MSCI USA index. Concentration is even more pronounced in certain Asian markets, with Samsung and TSMC holding 20% and 40% of their respective indices. While this trend has historically correlated with strong returns during rising markets, it also raises the risk of sharp declines if a few leading companies underperform. Many investors may believe they are diversified through index funds, but in reality, passive investment inflows disproportionately benefit the largest stocks, creating what analysts call a 'passive concentration trap.' Despite the high concentration, fundamentals of top firms remain strong, contributing significantly to overall market returns. Moreover, concentration does not uniformly increase volatility; it varies by region. Governments may also play a role in supporting national champions, further reinforcing market concentration. As markets become top-heavy, assessing risk and diversification becomes more complex, signalling a potential need for investors to reconsider traditional assumptions about market structure.
external.title: Rising Stock Market Concentration Driven by Tech Giants and AI

record.text:
HSBC boosts S&P 500 forecast as strong earnings and AI momentum support outlook

🤖 IA: It's not clickbait ✅
👥 Users: It's not clickbait ✅

#stockmarket #earningsgrowth #aiinvestments 

View full AI summary:

external.description:
HSBC has increased its year-end target for the S&P 500 index to 7,650, up from a previous estimate of 7,500, reflecting continued confidence in corporate earnings growth. The revised forecast suggests a modest upside of about 3.4% from the index’s recent close of 7,398.93. The upgrade comes amid a strong rally in U.S. equities, driven largely by optimism surrounding artificial intelligence investments and expectations of sustained profit expansion.

Recent market performance has been notable, with the S&P 500 recording its strongest monthly gain since November 2020 in April. HSBC analysts project earnings per share growth of approximately 20% in 2026, reaching around $325. A significant portion of this growth is expected to come from the so-called “Magnificent Seven” megacap technology firms, which continue to dominate market performance and investor attention.

First-quarter earnings have also exceeded expectations, with growth tracking close to 29% year over year, according to LSEG I/B/E/S data. Much of this surge has been attributed to major technology companies benefiting from AI-related demand. Despite these strong fundamentals, HSBC cautions that overall market sentiment remains somewhat fragile, noting that the recent rally has been narrow, with many stocks still trading below their 52-week highs.

The bank suggests there is potential for broader market participation, which could support further gains. In a more optimistic scenario, the S&P 500 could surpass 8,000 points if higher technology valuations, stronger initial public offerings, broader AI-driven earnings growth across sectors, and a supportive macroeconomic environment align. However, risks remain, including geopolitical tensions and inflation concerns linked to rising oil prices.
external.title: HSBC boosts S&P 500 forecast as strong earnings and AI momentum support outlook

Reflecting on Independence and Community: A Personal Journey Back Home

🤖 IA: It's not clickbait ✅
👥 Users: It's not clickbait ✅

#independence #community #lgbtq+ 

View full AI summary:

external.description: Jumol Royes moved from Vaughan, Ontario, to Ottawa in 2023 to pursue a long-deferred bachelor's degree in communications, seeking independence and a fresh start in midlife. As a Black gay man, he hoped to build a new life and reconnect with the 2SLGBTQ+ social scene. However, he quickly realized that Ottawa’s queer community was tightly knit and predominantly white, leaving him feeling isolated and invisible. His mental health suffered, and he even had to pause his studies. During this period, the death of his godmother prompted an extended visit home, where he reconnected with family and recognized the value of the emotional support they offered. Eventually, he decided to return to Vaughan permanently, embracing the community and lifelong relationships that had always been part of his life. Royes reflects that independence without a supportive community can become isolating and that real courage sometimes means acknowledging the importance of connection and support. He continues to work on completing his degree while rebuilding his sense of belonging and self-understanding in his home environment.
external.title: Reflecting on Independence and Community: A Personal Journey Back Home

Dear authors. 

If you produce an audiobook version, please don't have a professional narrator read it to us. We "readers" much prefer it if you read it yourself. 

Oh, and if your publisher wants to use an AI voice instead, know that they (and therefore you) will never ever see a penny from me.

AI's output is the 'average' of the internet. 

Read that again.

If we let AI do our thinking, we end up sounding exactly like our competitors. Because that's what AI is pulling from.

But when we sound like everyone else, the only thing left to compete on is price.

Be careful what you wish for.

To all those tech people who think marketing can be replaced by an AI function:

The actual job of marketing is to make people interested. 

Its not to find already-interested people and take credit for the sale of something that probably would have happened anyway.
